content: Many topics are so far over my head it isn't funny. So can folks share how long have you worked with CT? Also a rough timeline of when you pick up different major concepts would be nice. Thanks, Daniel.
(Moved to #general: off-topic)

Daniel Geisler said:

Many topics are so far over my head it isn't funny. So can folks share how long have you worked with CT? Also a rough timeline of when you pick up different major concepts would be nice. Thanks, Daniel.

35 years if you count from the fist little writeup solving something (MSc thesis). 37 if you count the first book you do all exercises in (johnstones first topos theory).

but i don't think category theory consists of "major concepts". there are accomplished category theorists who share very few major concepts: eg one works in functional programming, the other one in descent theory, the third one in quantum stuff. it is a language, just like english or german. you don't learn concepts, or grammar, but listen, and talk, and don't pay attention if anyone laughs.
I think there are some major concepts in category theory: limit&colimit, adjoint functor, monad, Kan extension, enriched category, ....

I learned a bunch of very basic category theory when studying homological algebra in grad school 1982-1986 - mainly stuff that shows up for abelian categories, like diagram chasing and derived functors. But I wasn't very excited about categories per se back then. I was mainly trying to understand algebraic topology & ring theory, not category theory (a mistake, I later realized).

I got into braided monoidal categories when the quantum group / knot theory / Chern-Simons theory hit mathematical physics in 1989, thanks to the work of Vaughan Jones and Ed Witten and later scores of others.

I was excited about how 3d topological quantum field theories arose from braided monoidal categories because it seemed to offer a new paradigm for thinking about quantum gravity - but just quantum gravity in 3d spacetime. Then Louis Crane and David Yetter argued that braided monoidal 2-categories could be good for quantum gravity in 4d spacetime, in 1993.
I started writing This Week's Finds about that stuff... and James Dolan got interested and came to U.C. Riverside, maybe around 1994, and he taught me a lot about category theory.
He really taught me to think like a pure mathematician. Before then I could only get really interested in math if I thought it was connected to physics.
I remember him teaching me about monads in a weird little restaurant... I thought they were way too abstract.

He was very good at explaining things in a down-to-earth, fun way. I used to think that to master category theory you need a guru, because the books were so dry compared to the actual subject. It seems to be getting better now.
agreed on there being major concepts
it's true that accomplished category theorists may apply category theory in very different areas, but that doesn't mean they don't use a lot of the same concepts in those different areas
if anything, i think part of the point of category theory is that it has major concepts that apply so well in such different areas!

John Baez said:

I think there are some major concepts in category theory: limit&colimit, adjoint functor, monad, Kan extension, enriched category, ....

i said "there are no concepts" more in response to the question about "the timeline to pick up major concepts". it's discouraging to say that there is a major concept and that it takes between 5 and 12 years to pick it up.

but the subtitle of the paper i just posted is: "CONCEPT mining from adjunctions" and the context is that adjunctions are the categorical rule to rule them all.
yeah i see yr point about discouragement

and then are limits and colimits categorical concepts? in john's language, they are categorifications of meets and joins. if we admit all categorifications as concepts of category theory, then few concepts will be left out. if we don't, then few concepts will be in. it is hard to pin a concept to a science.
i mean... yes, i would say that most categorifications are concepts of category theory
not all of them are major concepts :)
Limits and colimits, to me, are paradigmatic of categorical concepts. They are things that make sense in a category. They're also extremely important.

But they sure don't take 5 or 12 years to learn! You can get the idea in something between an hour and a week if you know what a category is and know a bit about how to think like a mathematician. All my current crop of students use them pretty fluently.

So how long did it take to understand that monad stuff? We're sharing our timelines here.... Dusko and I have been at this for too long to be inspiring to someone who wants to see if they can pick up category pretty quickly.
wait why is this 2 topics
zulip should really let you move topics between streams :persevere: or is it possible and i misunderstood?

I guess I will reply to the original question, since I just got here (hi everybody!). I first got into category theory when I read Pierce's book "Category Theory for Computer Scientists" in the early 00s, thinking it might be somehow useful for formalising something called "type enforcement" in OS security. Never had much success there. :)

As far as the core concepts of CT, I keep learning them over and over, because there's lots of different ways to think about them. For example, if you like to draw string diagrams and think about functors as wires, distributive laws are "crossing wires over each other" (see e.g. https://arxiv.org/pdf/1401.7220.pdf by @Dan Marsden). This is the only way I can consistently reconstruct all of the parts of the definition from memory (particularly when it comes to distributive laws of (co)monads). But then, you can also think about them as distributivity in the sense of a(b+c) = ab + ac, or more philosophically fancy (and less understood by me) things like "the interaction between computation and observation".

I think it's more useful to think about getting some skill in using some concepts and expanding your "bag of tricks", rather than a binary "understanding" vs "not understanding". For instance, their are some people who are total kung fu masters at universal properties, taking co-ends and Kan extending stuff all over the place, etc etc. I tend to get pretty lost in that kind of thing, but I can do some pretty fancy string diagram acrobatics.

Hi, I think I heard about CT in the 1980ies, but it was still known as abstract nonsense at the time, so even though I liked mathematics I found it difficult to motivate myself there, and instead read a lot of modal logic (David Lewis in particular) as part of Analytic Philosophy. I later got back to computing, learnt Java, went to California and ended up work on the BabelFish machine translation service at AltaVista.
Then from 2004 onwards worked at Sun Microsystems on blogging and the semantic web, and around 2010 I started looking at Scala, where the word Monads made it's appearance, which made me wonder if they were related to Leibniz. Actually I was wondering for a while if they could not be used for logical security contexts (which I discovered later they can as shown by Martin Abadi). I discovered Free Monads and found them very useful for writing a read-write web server based on Actor architecture. So slowly I thought I should learn more about CT. I found the book Conceptual Mathematics: A First Introduction to Categories by Lawvere around 2014 but it was very slow, so I could not see the point of it. A bit later I got the book Category Theory by Awodey, that moved much faster but it looked like rocket science, and all the examples were from maths, where I had no idea how long it could take me to learn them. At a Scala conference in London around 2016, I mentioned Lawvere's book to Martin Odersky the creator of Scala and he somehow mentioned HoTT as the big revolutionary change in maths. Thinking CT was it, I was very surprised and later read the first few chapters of HoTT which I found very well written. But the modal story did not feel that good. (and having recently read Modal HoTT I know why).
In any case, I started to have questions as to how OO programming, FP, the semantic web, protocols and all that fit together, mostly because over time I was getting the impression that some very intelligent people could not see some very interesting advantages in other areas, as if there were major conceptual barriers around, some categorical structures people could not overcome, so to speak. Somehow after building an EU consortium with Oxford, Southampton, INRIA, EPFL, and business partners such as Imogen Heap, Catapult and a number of startups to build an open decentralised secure platform built on Tim Berners-Lee's Solid architecture, I ended up at Southampton, where I am writing a thesis on Web Architecture and Security, looking for mathematics to ground the work.
There I discovered that Modal Logics are Colagebraic and so is OO programming, through Bart Jacobs articles (see this Quora answer) things started to become a lot more interesting. In 2016 I found a thesis by Benjamin Braatz giving a Category Theorectic view of RDF which was also very helpful for me to understand CT. Recently I found a lot more on the subject having started a group Web Cats, to see how category theory can be applied to the web. On the whole as I discovered more and more introductions to CT from a programming perspective (e.g. Bartosz Milewski's courses and book I found it easier and easier to see the uses of it and so to learn it. So much so that I recently looked at Awodey's book at realized that I had learnt nearly all the concepts in it). Indeed I now feel like I can use CT to get into mathematics :-)
What I like the most about CT is its ability to reveal dualities. That should be the topic of the year 2020.
I also write more philosophical, non-CT related blog posts. My latest is Co-Immunology and the Web.
@Henry Story Interesting, the article, Modal Logics are Coalgebraic, you mentioned is coauthored by my PhD supervisor, Dirk Pattinson, but I never had a chance to discuss category theory with him.
This topic was moved here from #general > How long have you worked with CT? by Nathanael Arkor
so we can move topics between streams now?
Yes.
